data.title: Plains GP targets $2.8B–$2.95B 2025 EBITDA as NGL divestiture drives crude oil focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Plains GP Holdings (PAGP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Wilfred C.W. Chiang, Chairman, CEO & President, highlighted the execution of definitive agreements to sell substantially all of Plains' NGL business to Keyera for approximately $3.75 billion, with closing expected in Q1 2026. Chiang stated the sale would result in a streamlined crude oil midstream entity with less commodity exposure and more durable cash flow, providing "substantial financial flexibility to further execute on our capital allocation framework."
  • Chiang emphasized the redeployment of approximately $3 billion in net proceeds from the NGL sale into disciplined bolt-on M&A and optimizing capital structure, including potential repurchases of preferred and common units. He announced a bolt-on acquisition of an additional 20% interest in BridgeTex Pipeline Company LLC for $100 million, bringing Plains' total interest in the joint venture to 40%.
  • Al P. Swanson, Executive VP & CFO, noted the reclassification of the majority of the NGL segment as discontinued operations. Swanson reported, "We reported crude Oil segment adjusted EBITDA of $580 million, which benefited sequentially from Permian volume growth, contributions from recent bolt-on acquisitions and higher throughput associated with our refining customers returning from downtime in the first quarter of 2025."
  • Swanson added, "Our full year 2025 EBITDA range of $2.8 billion to $2.95 billion remains intact. Consistent with our communication last quarter, in the prevailing environment, both our EBITDA guidance and the Permian growth outlook of 200,000 to 300,000 barrels per day would likely be in the lower half of their respective ranges."

Outlook

  • The company maintained its full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $2.8 billion to $2.95 billion. Management reiterated that both EBITDA and Permian growth outlooks are expected to be in the lower half of their respective ranges under the current environment.
  • Swanson stated, "For 2025, we expect to generate approximately $870 million of adjusted free cash flow, excluding changes in assets and liabilities."
  • Growth capital guidance for 2025 increased by $75 million to $475 million, with the rise attributed to new projects including Permian and South Texas lease connects and terminal expansions, as well as weather delays and scope changes.

Financial Results

  • Plains reported Q2 adjusted EBITDA attributable to Plains of $672 million.
  • Crude Oil segment adjusted EBITDA was $580 million, benefiting from Permian volume growth, bolt-on acquisitions, and increased throughput from refining customers returning from downtime.
  • NGL segment adjusted EBITDA was $87 million, stepping down sequentially due to normal seasonality and lower quarter-on-quarter frac spreads.
  • Maintenance capital is trending closer to $230 million, which is $10 million below initial forecast.
  • Year-to-date, five bolt-on transactions totaling approximately $800 million have been completed.

Q&A

  • Shneur Z. Gershuni, UBS: Asked about sensitivity to basin-level growth for bolt-on strategies and which basins are expected to show more growth. Jeremy L. Goebel responded that all considerations, including basin fundamentals, are factored into discounted cash flow analysis and return thresholds: "We're not necessarily going to say where our target area is right now, but we do look at everything, and we've got to hit our return thresholds."
  • Gershuni also inquired about refining and export demand signals. Goebel replied, "We haven't seen the slowdown in demand most were expecting. And we expect that to continue."
  • Gabriel Philip Moreen, Mizuho: Asked about BridgeTex's contractual situation and how it fits with Plains' business. Goebel described working with ONEOK to optimize cost structure and pipeline capacity, aiming to strengthen positioning long term.
  • Moreen followed up on CapEx increases. Chris Chandler explained the rise is due to new Permian and Eagle Ford gathering opportunities and additional storage, noting these investments "exceed our return thresholds."
  • Michael Jacob Blum, Wells Fargo: Inquired if the company's plan is to continue its growth and capital return focus or consider diversifying. Chiang responded that value creation remains the objective and redeployment of NGL sale proceeds will focus on bolt-ons and enhancing the liquids business.
  • Blum also asked about a change in distribution growth messaging. Swanson clarified, "No intended shift in messaging at all. We intend to grow our distribution over a multiyear period."
  • Spiro Michael Dounis, Citi: Sought clarity on second half 2025 expectations. Goebel referenced contract roll-offs and recontracting at lower rates, resulting in backfilled volumes but flat performance.
  • Dounis asked about the appetite for larger deals post-NGL sale. Chiang pointed to increased financial flexibility and the ability to pursue opportunities of varying sizes.
  • Sunil Sibal, Seaport Global: Asked if the BridgeTex acquisition is part of the Oryx JV (it is not), and about retained US NGL assets. Goebel said these are minor and likely to be divested later.
  • John Ross Mackay, Goldman Sachs: Queried CapEx increase drivers. Chandler attributed it to both new opportunities and bolt-on synergy capture, with a portion being deferrals from last year. Goebel quantified retained NGL EBITDA at $10 million to $15 million, with assets valued at $100 million to $200 million.
  • Brandon B. Bingham, Scotiabank: Sought clarity on EBITDA guidance language. Swanson confirmed guidance to the "lower half," not the bottom end.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised several questions about capital deployment, growth strategy, and CapEx increases, maintaining a neutral tone with some focus on clarity regarding future capital allocation and guidance language.
  • Management responded with confidence, emphasizing discipline, financial flexibility, and a constructive long-term outlook. Chiang stated, "I'm confident in our position," and Swanson clarified guidance language with assurance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions shifted from macro volatility and M&A landscape to the specifics of capital redeployment and strategic focus post-NGL divestiture. Management appeared more confident discussing flexibility and strategic direction, while analysts remained pragmatic and sought detail on execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's call focused heavily on the NGL divestiture, its proceeds, and the resulting strategic pivot to a more concentrated crude oil midstream business. This is a shift from last quarter's discussion of market volatility, NGL project completions, and continued bolt-on M&A.
  • Guidance for full-year 2025 EBITDA remained at $2.8 billion to $2.95 billion, but management now expects results in the lower half of the range, similar to last quarter's cautious language. Permian growth outlook of 200,000 to 300,000 barrels per day is reiterated with the same lower-half expectation.
  • CapEx guidance increased this quarter due to new growth opportunities and weather-related project delays, compared to the previous quarter's unchanged guidance.
  • Analyst focus has evolved from risk management and macro uncertainty to more specific questions on capital reallocation and growth strategy execution following the NGL sale.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, with a slight increase in clarity and decisiveness when addressing future strategy and use of proceeds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged near-term market volatility and the risk of lower-than-expected growth, but emphasized confidence in long-term fundamentals.
  • The NGL segment's sequential EBITDA decline was attributed to seasonality and lower frac spreads, highlighting ongoing commodity exposure risk until the sale closes.
  • Analysts queried about execution risks related to capital redeployment, contract roll-offs, and the ability to source attractive bolt-on deals.
  • Management cited discipline in capital allocation, maintaining financial flexibility, and a continued focus on risk-adjusted returns as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Plains GP Holdings signaled a clear strategic transformation with the pending $3.75 billion divestiture of its NGL business, sharpening its focus on crude oil midstream operations and unlocking significant financial flexibility. Management reiterated its commitment to disciplined capital allocation, a robust bolt-on acquisition strategy, and sustainable distribution growth, while maintaining guidance for 2025 EBITDA and free cash flow in the lower half of the stated ranges. The company is positioning itself to redeploy proceeds into value-accretive opportunities and optimize its capital structure, with confidence in long-term industry fundamentals despite near-term volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nerdy outlines sequential revenue and gross margin growth through 2025 with accelerating Live+AI adoption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nerdy, Inc. (NRDY) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Charles Cohn, CEO, highlighted "meaningful progress on our Live+AI vision over the past quarter as we work to embed real-time intelligence in each stage of the learning journey," with enhancements leading to "stronger engagement and retention in new cohorts that position us for sustained growth this coming school year and beyond." Learning Membership revenue returned to year-over-year growth, with a 4% increase, driven by more engaged customers and AI-powered product improvements. Varsity Tutors for Schools bookings grew 21% year-over-year, supported by high dosage tutoring demand and positive feedback on the Live+AI platform. Cohn stated, "We continue to expect consumer revenue to accelerate in growth each quarter throughout 2025."
  • The CEO described a series of product enhancements, including interactive video playback, AI-generated session summaries, AI-powered Learner-Expert match explanations, automated calendar invites, and streamlined mobile-friendly checkout. A major overhaul of practice resources was completed, evolving them into modern AI-powered interactive learning experiences covering hundreds of subjects.
  • Cohn announced the launch of the desktop Nerdy app for tutors, providing "real-time guidance to the tutor on a heads-up translucent display" and supporting both tutors and students with context-aware AI features. For institutional clients, more than 30 new AI tools will be available to educators this back-to-school season, automating tasks and enhancing personalization.
  • Cohn shared that in a recent partnership with a top 10 U.S. school district, students using high-dosage tutoring saw an average 11-point improvement from midyear to year-end and significant gains in early literacy and state assessment scores.
  • Jason H. Pello, CFO, stated, "Second quarter revenue was in line with expectations, delivering revenue of $45.3 million, within our guidance range of $45 million to $48 million." He also shared, "Gross margin increased 350 basis points sequentially from the first quarter as a result of price increases for new customers enacted during the first quarter of 2025."

Outlook

  • Pello guided for consolidated and consumer revenues to "return to being positive year-over-year" in Q3 2025, driven by higher customer acquisition and improved ARPM. For Q3, revenue is expected in the range of $37 million to $40 million, and full-year revenue is projected at $191 million to $197 million. Institutional revenue is expected to return to year-over-year growth in Q4 2025.
  • Pello stated, "We expect to deliver sequential quarterly gross margin improvements."
  • Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss for Q3 is forecast at $11 million to $13 million, and for the full year at $13 million to $17 million. The company expects to be profitable on a non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA basis in Q4 2025, ending the year with cash in the range of $30 million to $35 million and no debt.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q2 2025 was $45.3 million, a decrease of 11% year-over-year, attributed to the absence of a non-recurring state-funded program and lower institutional revenue. Consumer Learning Membership revenue reached $37.8 million, representing 84% of total revenue. Active Members as of June 30 were 30,600, and ARPM was $348, up 24% year-over-year.
  • Institutional business reported $7.3 million in revenue, 16% of total revenue. Varsity Tutors for Schools secured 50 contracts with $4.9 million in bookings, a 21% year-over-year increase.
  • Gross margin expanded by 350 basis points sequentially. Sales and marketing expenses declined to $13.5 million from $15.5 million in the prior year quarter. Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss was $2.7 million, which was better than the top end of guidance.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $36.7 million as of June 30, with zero debt.

Q&A

  • Bryan Michael Smilek, JPMorgan: Asked about confidence in revenue growth and the composition of active member versus ARPM growth. Cohn responded, "The last 60 days, in particular, were the single most productive period in our company history by a significant margin," citing improvements in retention, onboarding, and conversion. Pello added that ARPM is expected to increase to $380 in Q3 and Q4, with Active Members reaching 38,000 in Q3 and 40,000 by year-end.
  • Jason Ross Tilchen, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about the most impactful AI tools. Cohn highlighted the Nerdy app's real-time AI features for tutors and students, and agentic practice problems that generate personalized content after sessions.
  • Yi Fu Lee, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about industry AI momentum, and Cohn noted, "There's no longer fear of using AI. There's an understanding that not only is it permissible, but it's expected and even required." Lee also asked about high-dosage teaching rollout, and Pello stated the institutional opportunity is "very real" and Q3 is shaping up to be the largest bookings quarter yet.
  • Gregory Thomas Gibas, Northland Securities: Asked about early back-to-school engagement trends and EBITDA outperformance. Cohn cited improving conversion and retention trends, and Pello attributed EBITDA outperformance to gross margin improvement and efficiency gains from AI investments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on growth levers, early cohort trends, and the impact of AI initiatives, while seeking clarity on execution and rollout timelines. Tone was neutral to slightly positive, with constructive questions.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Cohn described recent months as "the single most productive period in our company history," and Pello discussed sequential improvements and reaffirmed profitability targets.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence was more pronounced, with greater enthusiasm for product momentum, while analysts maintained a similar constructive tone.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company moved from a focus on returning to growth and improving foundational metrics in Q1 to delivering tangible Learning Membership revenue growth and higher engagement in Q2.
  • Guidance evolved from ARPM targets above $370 to specific projections for ARPM and Active Members through year-end, and full-year revenue guidance was refined.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious confidence to overt enthusiasm, citing a "productive period" and accelerated innovation.
  • Analysts continued to probe for visibility into growth levers and AI impact, with recurring themes on retention, onboarding, and product differentiation.
  • Key metrics such as ARPM, Active Members, and institutional bookings showed positive sequential trends, with gross margin expansion emerging more visibly in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted continued caution regarding federal and state institutional funding, with lower institutional revenue in the first half of the year.
  • The company is investing in AI and expert incentives, which could impact margins if not offset by revenue growth and operational efficiencies.
  • Analysts raised questions about the timing and sustainability of revenue acceleration, institutional rollout, and gross margin improvement.

Final Takeaway

Nerdy reported a return to Learning Membership revenue growth and significant progress on its Live+AI vision, supported by accelerating product innovation and operational efficiencies. Management emphasized sequential improvements in revenue, ARPM, and gross margin through 2025, with strong momentum entering the back-to-school season and a clear path to fourth quarter profitability and positive cash flow. The rollout of new AI-driven tools for tutors, students, and educators, combined with expanding institutional adoption, underpins management's confidence in sustained growth and strengthened market positioning.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: e.l.f. Beauty lists 2.5M merger-issued shares for sale by holders
data.description:
• E.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) filed a shelf registration with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission pertaining to the sale of ~2.5M shares by stockholders related to a merger agreement of HRBeauty, the parent company of rhode.
  • E.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) is not selling any shares under this prospectus and will not receive any proceeds from the sale by the selling stockholders.
  • Under the terms of the merger agreement, e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) agreed to acquire rhode for $800 million at closing, comprised of $600 million of cash and $200 million, or approximately 2.5 million shares, of newly issued shares of e.l.f. Beauty common stock issued to existing equity holders of rhode,
  • In accordance with the company’s obligations under the merger agreement, e.l.f. (ELF) agreed to register the resale of these shares offered by the selling shareholders.

data.title: Ocugen launches $20 million direct offering of common stock, warrants
data.description:
• Ocugen (NASDAQ:OCGN) has entered into a securities purchase agreement with Janus Henderson Investors to purchase 20M shares of common stock and warrants for gross proceeds of $20 million
  • The company may receive up to $30 million of additional gross proceeds if the warrants are exercised in full.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about August 11, 2025.
  • OCGN -1.83% after hours to $0.9801.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: ATN International reaffirms 2025 guidance and raises dividend 15% amid ongoing fiber expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ATN International, Inc. (ATNI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Brad W. Martin, CEO, began by emphasizing continued progress on long-term strategic priorities and noted that "our Q2 results were in line with our expectations and reflect the steps we're taking to optimize our cost structure." He highlighted a 1% revenue decline year-over-year, a 6% decrease in adjusted EBITDA, and a 2% rise in net cash from operations to approximately $60 million.
  • Martin announced, "our Board of Directors approved a 15% increase in our quarterly dividend, raising it up to $0.275 per share. This reflects the confidence we have in the underlying strength and resilience of our cash flow."
  • Martin reported an 8% increase in broadband homes passed by high-speed data services and a 1% growth in the high-speed subscriber base year-over-year. He stated the company is "capitalizing on our fiber, fiber-fed and broader network infrastructure to deliver essential high-value services in underserved markets."
  • In the International segment, Martin noted "postpaid subscribers grew by 4% year-over-year," and customers purchasing and consuming data plans rose by 25% year-over-year. Mobile churn rates improved by more than 5% for the second quarter in a row, and high-speed data ARPU and subscriber churn both improved by 3% year-over-year.
  • For the U.S. segment, Martin indicated "consumer broadband subscriber base grew more than 10% this quarter, and we are seeing positive trends in ARPU." He also highlighted ongoing investments in over $300 million in broadband infrastructure initiatives backed by government funding, with more than half slated for completion in 2025.
  • Carlos R. Doglioli, CFO, stated, "Total company revenue for the quarter was $181.3 million, down 1% year-over-year... Operating income for the second quarter decreased to $0.2 million versus $24.3 million in the year ago period."

Outlook

  • Martin reaffirmed the company's full year guidance as outlined during the prior quarter's call, stating, "we are reaffirming our full year guidance as outlined during our fourth quarter earnings call."
  • Doglioli specified, "We continue to expect revenue for the year to be in line with 2024 of $725 million, excluding construction revenue, adjusted EBITDA to be essentially flat with last year's $184 million. Net capital expenditures between $90 million and $100 million, down from $110.3 million in 2024 and net debt ratios to remain flat to year-end 2024 with a slight potential improvement exiting the year."
  • Management expects the second half to contribute a larger share of full year results, and residual restructuring and reorganization expenses in Q3, but at a lower level than Q2.

Financial Results

  • Doglioli reported, "Total company revenue for the quarter was $181.3 million, down 1% year-over-year." Operating income was $0.2 million, which compares to $24.3 million in the prior year, a period that included a $15.9 million gain from asset disposition.
  • Net loss for the quarter was $7 million or $0.56 per share, compared to net income of $9 million or $0.50 per share in the prior year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $45.8 million, down 6% from the prior year, mainly due to the impact of lower U.S. Telecom revenues.
  • The company ended the quarter with $113.3 million in cash and total debt of $583.4 million. Net cash provided by operating activities for the first half of the year increased 2% to approximately $60 million.
  • Capital expenditures for the first half of 2025 totaled $42 million, net of $45.9 million in reimbursable capital spend.

Q&A

  • Richard Hamilton Prentiss, Raymond James: Asked about the impact of the BBB bill. Doglioli replied, "it hasn't had an impact. Given our jurisdictions and tax positions, we're not expecting an impact in the short term."
  • Prentiss inquired about competition and access to labor/materials in fiber deployment. Martin responded, "those policies to help -- really help expedite permitting are going to be very helpful for us... So we do expect that's going to help speed up pace." On labor, Martin added, "we're seeing people coming to us quite regularly looking for work. So, no, we've not seen that yet."
  • Prentiss asked about the potential for converting the fiber business into a REIT. Martin answered, "that's not something we've looked at... there are other mechanisms out there, asset-backed securitization... But from a REIT perspective, not yet."
  • Gregory John Burns, Sidoti: Asked about inflection in U.S. fiber-led service growth. Martin replied, "we are optimistic with the progress we've made with our fiber builds, and we are seeing demand from the carriers starting to increase... we are expecting to continue to see improvements here in the second half of the year."
  • Burns also asked about competitive dynamics internationally. Martin noted, "the initial new entrant in our largest market, that was disruptive last year... has normalized to a degree... the competitive pressures are still there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, focused on understanding impacts of legislation, competition, and growth inflection points, with no overt skepticism or negative sentiment.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing execution, cost control, and optimism for the second half. Martin stated, "We are focused on disciplined execution grounded in financial responsibility and confident in the strategic path we've chosen."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained steady with a focus on operational progress and future growth, though management showed slightly more confidence with the dividend increase and progress on strategic projects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with the outlook reaffirmed and expectations for the second half underscored in both quarters.
  • Strategic focus continues on cost control, operational efficiency, and fiber expansion, with a notable increase in dividend and more detailed updates on infrastructure initiatives this quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on subsidy program impacts, monetization of investments, and competitive dynamics.
  • Key metrics such as revenue and adjusted EBITDA were similar quarter-over-quarter, though Q2 saw increased restructuring charges and marginal growth in cash flow.
  • Management's confidence level remained stable, with slightly more positive forward-looking commentary this quarter, especially regarding U.S. fiber build progress and dividend actions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the wind down of subsidy programs and decommissioning of legacy services as key headwinds impacting revenue.
  • Ongoing restructuring and reorganization charges were noted, with expectations for lower charges in Q3.
  • Competitive pressures, especially in international prepaid mobile markets, remain an area of focus.
  • Management is monitoring policy changes, trade, and tariff developments, stating they "remain confident in our ability to absorb any short-term effects within the parameters of our current 2025 financial guidance."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that ATN International is executing with discipline, reaffirming its 2025 financial guidance while raising the quarterly dividend by 15% as a sign of confidence in its cash flow and strategic direction. The company continues to prioritize operational efficiency, network expansion—particularly in fiber—and stabilization of its U.S. and International segments, positioning itself for improved performance in the second half of the year and sustainable long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Soluna announces CFO transition
data.description:
• Soluna (NASDAQ:SLNH) announced on Friday that it has accepted the resignation of John Tunison, chief financial officer and treasurer, effective August 21st.
  • The company said Tunison is departing from his role at Soluna to accept a similar position with a privately held company.
  • David Michaels, a longtime board member filling various roles at the company over his tenure, including chairman of the board, lead independent director, and chairman of the audit committee, has assumed the role of Interim CFO and Treasurer. Michaels previously served as interim CFO of the company in 2023
  • SLNH +1.49% after hours to $0.4699.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Petrobras plunges after pouring cold water on extra dividend hopes
data.description:
Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) closed -7.3% on Friday after posting weaker than expected Q2 results and warning it sees little chance of paying extraordinary dividends this year.

Q2 swung to a net profit of 26.65B reais (~$4.9B) from a net loss of 2.61B reais in the year-earlier quarter, when results were affected by a major tax dispute, but net revenues fell 2.6% Y/Y to 119.13B reais, as the company said Brent oil prices averaged $67.82/bbl in the quarter, compared to $75.66/bbl in Q1.

Q2 total hydrocarbon production jumped 5% Q/Q to 2.91M bbl/day, and the company maintained full-year guidance of 2.8M boe/day with 4% leeway.

Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) announced dividends and interest on equity to shareholders of 8.66B reais (~$1.6B), but CFO Fernando Melgarejo said on its earnings conference call.

"Although we would very much like to have the excess cash to make an extraordinary payment, we see a low probability this year," the CFO said, citing lower revenues because of weaker global oil prices.

Petrobras' (PBR) Q2 capital spending totaled $4.41B, mainly at large projects in the Santos Basin, and Melgarejo reaffirmed the company's $18.5B capital spending outlook for the full year, but added that some projects could be revised to take the lower oil prices into account.
